model_narrative: We plan motor production across a sequence of seasons. In each season we decide how many motors to produce, which shifts to run, how many employees to put on each shift, how many employees to hire or fire, how much inventory to carry into the next season, and whether to take an extra space lease for the horizon. Production on a shift follows a piecewise relationship between the labor used and the motors produced, expressed by choosing weights over a set of production levels. Costs accrue from materials, running shifts and paying shift labor, carrying inventory and renting overflow space, and hiring and firing employees, and each season's costs are discounted to present value. The objective is to minimize the total discounted cost over the whole horizon.

sets.name: q
sets.doc: the production seasons in chronological order; the first member is the opening season and each later member immediately follows the one before it

sets.name: s
sets.doc: the work shifts that can be run within a season

sets.name: l
sets.doc: the ordered production levels, also called breakpoints, used to describe a piecewise relationship between labor used and motors produced on a shift; listed from lowest to highest

params.name: d
params.doc: the motor demand that must be met in each season, in motors

params.name: lc
params.doc: the leasing cost charged in each season if the extra space lease is taken, in dollars

params.name: delt
params.doc: the discount factor applied to each season's costs to convert them to present value; dimensionless

params.name: mc
params.doc: the material cost per motor produced, in dollars per motor

params.name: sr
params.doc: the space rental cost charged per unit of inventory held beyond the leased allowance, in dollars per motor

params.name: hc
params.doc: the hiring cost per employee hired, in dollars per employee

params.name: fc
params.doc: the firing cost per employee fired, in dollars per employee

params.name: invmax
params.doc: the inventory allowance that the space lease covers, equal to total demand across all seasons, in motors

params.name: pr_motor
params.doc: the breakpoint table for the piecewise labor-to-motor relationship; the 'labor' row gives the labor amount at each production level and the 'motor' row gives the motors produced at each production level, so a chosen mix over the levels yields a matching pair of labor and motors

params.name: sc
params.doc: the shift cost table; the 'fixed' row gives the fixed cost incurred when a shift is run and the 'labor' row gives the cost per employee on that shift, in dollars

vars.name: cost
vars.doc: the total discounted cost over the whole horizon, the quantity being minimized, in thousands of dollars

vars.name: dpc
vars.doc: the direct production cost in each season, in thousands of dollars

vars.name: isc
vars.doc: the inventory cost in each season, in thousands of dollars

vars.name: wfc
vars.doc: the workforce change cost in each season, in thousands of dollars

vars.name: src
vars.doc: the space rental charge in each season, in dollars

vars.name: p
vars.doc: the number of motors produced in each season

vars.name: ss
vars.doc: the weight placed on each production level for a season and shift; these weights select a point along the piecewise labor-to-motor breakpoints

vars.name: ssb
vars.doc: an auxiliary 0/1 selector associated with each production level on a season and shift

vars.name: inv
vars.doc: the motors held in inventory at the end of each season

vars.name: lease
vars.doc: a single 0/1 decision that equals 1 if the extra space lease is taken for the horizon and 0 otherwise

vars.name: e
vars.doc: the total number of employees on the payroll in each season

vars.name: se
vars.doc: the number of employees working on each shift in each season

vars.name: shift
vars.doc: a 0/1 indicator that equals 1 if the shift is run in the season and 0 otherwise

vars.name: h
vars.doc: the number of employees hired in each season

vars.name: f
vars.doc: the number of employees fired in each season

constraints.name: acost
constraints.intent: The total discounted cost ties together every season's costs. For each season add up its direct production cost, its inventory cost, and its workforce change cost, scale that sum by the season's discount factor, and set the total cost equal to these discounted season totals added across all seasons.

constraints.name: ddpc
constraints.intent: The direct production cost of a season comes from making motors and from running and staffing shifts. For each season it equals the material cost for every motor produced, plus for each shift the fixed cost charged when that shift is run together with the per employee labor cost applied to the employees on that shift, with the whole amount expressed in thousands of dollars.

constraints.name: sbp
constraints.intent: Production in a season is set by how the chosen weights fall across the production levels on each shift. For each season the motors produced equal the sum over both shifts and all production levels of the motors associated with each level times the weight placed on that level.

constraints.name: sbse
constraints.intent: The employees on a shift follow from where the chosen weights fall across the production levels for that shift. For each season and each shift the shift employment equals the sum over all production levels of the labor associated with each level times the weight placed on that level.

constraints.name: scc
constraints.intent: The weights placed across the production levels on a shift are only available when that shift is actually run. For each season and each shift the weights across all production levels add up to the run indicator of that shift, so they sum to one when the shift is run and to zero otherwise.

constraints.name: invb
constraints.intent: Inventory carries motors from one season into the next. For each season the ending inventory equals the inventory left from the immediately preceding season plus the motors produced that season minus the demand met that season. In the very first season there is no preceding season, so nothing carries in.

constraints.name: disc
constraints.intent: The inventory cost of a season combines the leasing charge and the space rental charge. For each season it equals the season's leasing cost counted only when the lease is taken, plus that season's space rental charge, with the whole amount expressed in thousands of dollars.

constraints.name: dsrc
constraints.intent: The space rental charge covers inventory held beyond what the lease allowance covers. For each season the rental charge must be at least the per unit rental rate applied to the inventory held that season reduced by the lease allowance when the lease is taken.

constraints.name: dwfc
constraints.intent: The workforce change cost of a season comes from hiring and firing. For each season it equals the hiring cost for every employee hired plus the firing cost for every employee fired, with the whole amount expressed in thousands of dollars.

constraints.name: ed
constraints.intent: Total employment in a season is shared across its shifts. For each season the total number of employees equals the employees summed over all shifts.

constraints.name: eb2
constraints.intent: The workforce changes from season to season only through hiring and firing. For each season the total employment equals the employment carried from the immediately preceding season plus the employees hired that season minus the employees fired that season. In the very first season there is no preceding season, so the carried employment is zero.

constraints.name: lss1
constraints.intent: The weights across the production levels on a shift must respect the ordering of the levels, so that weight is placed only on neighboring levels rather than scattered across distant ones. For each season, each shift, and each production level, the weight on that level together with the weight on the level just below it cannot exceed the weight on that level together with the weights on the two levels just below it. The level just below and the two levels just below are only counted when they exist within the ordering.

constraints.name: mess1
constraints.intent: Exactly one full unit of weight is spread across the production levels on every shift. For each season and each shift the weights across all production levels add up to one.

query: There's a proposal on the table to draw down our peak warehousing commitment for the year by holding the motors carried in stock across all four quarters to a combined 20000 over the cycle. We're weighing it — fold that annual carried-stock budget into the plan and tell me the resulting schedule and total cost.
expected_pyomo: model.annual_inv_budget = Constraint(expr=sum(model.inv[q] for q in model.q) <= 20000.0)

query: The whole year's stockpile is bunched into the winter quarter — by the close of winter we're sitting on more than half of all the motors we carry across the entire cycle. Shouldn't no single quarter hold more than half of the total stock carried over the year, so the warehousing load is spread instead of spiking? What's keeping the model from flattening that pile-up?
expected_pyomo: model.winter_stock_share = Constraint(expr=model.inv['winter'] <= 0.5*sum(model.inv[q] for q in model.q))

query: Two things about this plan rub HR and finance the wrong way at once. The crew balloons through the year — winter headcount runs more than forty over where summer started, way past the thirty-head swing the floor can absorb cleanly — and on top of that we've signed up for the outside warehouse lease again. Shouldn't we be able to hold the winter-versus-summer headcount swing to thirty and stay inside our own four walls without the lease, both together? What's the model seeing that makes it lean on both the hiring surge and the rented space?

constraints.intent: Exactly one full unit of weight is spread across the production levels on every shift. For each season and each shift the weights across all production levels add up to one.

query: Headcount keeps climbing as the year runs on, peaking near 87 on the floor by the back half — that's a tight squeeze on the space and supervisors we've got. Shouldn't every quarter be held to a standing crew of 75 so we're not overstuffing the floor in any single period? What's keeping the model from staffing within that ceiling?
